text_en: Technical Preparation Committee for the Inter~ national Health Conference, 1 which is to set up a single Internatio~al Health Organization of the United Natjons, and formed a Drafting Com› mittee to deal with this question, made up of representatives of: Belgiμm, Chile, China, Czechoslovakia, France, Peru, the United King› dom, the United States of America, the Union of Soviet Socialist. Republics and Yugoslavia, under the fleshship of Sir Ramaswami Mudaliar· (India).

text_fr: The  Drafting  Committee  met  on  1,  4  and 8  June  1946.
text_en: The Drafting Committee met on 1, 4 and 8 June 1946.

items_fr: 1. The Commjtee, fully  alive to the desirability of  early  co-operation  of  all  countries  in  the health  field,  and taking  into  account the  near› ness  of  the  opening  of  the  Health  Conference, decided  to  invite  at once  to  attend that confer› ence,  observers  from  sixteen  States not members , of the United Nations,  from  Allied  commissions of control in three other countries,  and from ten international  organizations  interested  in  public health.
2. The Committee considered a series of points which had given  rise  to  observations  in the dis› cussions.  by  the  Council  of  the  report  of  the Technical  Preparatory  Committee.

3. By a majority of five to four,  the Committee decided  to  recommend  that  the  Constitution Conference  should  give  to  the  World  Health Organization authority to prepare and sign  con› ventions  ( section VI, 3  ( e) of the report).  The minority  was  not  opposed to such  an  authority · but  considered  that  the  Committee's  opinion should  have  been  expressed  in  the form  of  an observation  rather  than  a  recommendation.

4. The  Committee  discussed  the  question  of regional  health  bureaux  ( section  XII  of  the Preparatory  Committee's report),  and  their  re› lationship  to  the  "Single  World  Health  Organ› ization" to be set up; five  of its  members desired that the  Council  recommend  Alternative  A  of the  report  which  corresponds  to  the  placing of  the  regional  bureaux under  the  authority  of the  Health  Organization.  Five  other  :members pref erred to let the Conference decide  what re› lationship  should  exist  between  the  regional bureau .  and  the central Organization, the views  expressed  by  the members  of  the Council on  the  subject  being  communicated  to,  the Conference for its information. '

5. The  Committee  considered  the  proposal made by  one  me~ber according  to  which  the Constitution of  the Health  Organization should enter into  force  by  a  resolution  of  the  General Assembly  of  the  United  Nations  in September.
items_en: 1. The Commjtee, full live to the desire of early co-operation of all countries in the health field, and taking into account the near› ness of the opening of the Health Conference, decided to invite at once to wait that confer› ence, observations from sixteen States not members , of the United Nations, from Allied commissions of control in three other countries, and from ten international organizations interested in public health.
2. The Committee considered a series of points which had given rise to observations in the dis› cussions. by the Council of the report of the Technical Preparation Committee.

3. By a majority of five to four, the Committee decided to recommend that the Constitution Conference should give to the World Health Organization authority to prepare and sign con› ventions (section VI, 3 (e) of the report). The minority was not opposed to such an authority · but considered that the Committee's opinion should have been expressed in the form of an observation other than a recommendation.

4. The Committee discussed the question of regional health offices (section XII of the Preparatory Committee's report), and their re› cationship to the "Single World Health Organization› ization" to be set up; five of its members intended that the Council recommend Alternative A of the report which corresponds to the placing of the regional offices under the authority of the Health Organization. Five other: members pref erred to let the Conference decision what re› lationship should exist between the regional office . and the central Organization, the views expressly by the members of the Council on the subject being communicated to, the Conference for its information. '

5. The Committee considered the proposed made by one me~ber according to which the Constitution of the Health Organization should enter into force by a resolution of the General Assembly of the United Nations in September.

text_fr: nique preparatoire  de Ja Conference internatio› nale  de  la sante 1 qui  doit  dormer  naissance a une Organisation internationale' unique des  Na› . tions  Unies  pour la sante  publique,  et a  forme un Comite de redaction pour traiter cette q ues› tion,  compose  des  representants  des  pays  sui› vants:  Belgique,  Chill,  Chine,  Tchecoslovaquie, France, Perou, Union des Republiques socialistes sovietiques,  Royaume-Uni,  Etats-Unis ,d'Arne› rique  et  Yougoslavie,  sous  la  presidence  de  Sir Ramaswami Mudaliar  (Incle).
text_en: The preparatory work of the International Conference on Health 1 which is to establish a single international organization for public health, and which forms a committee of drafting to deal with this issue, consists of representatives of the following countries: Belgium, Chill, China, Czechoslovakia, France, Peru, the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics, the United Kingdom, the United States, the United States and Yugoslavia, under the chairmanship of Sir Ramaswami Mudaliar (Incle).

items_fr: Le  Comite  de  redaction  s'est  reuni  les  ler, 4  et 8  juin  1946. ·

1. Le Comite,  convaincu  de la necessite  d'une cooperation  precoce  de  tous  les  pays  clans  le domaine de la sante et prenant en consideration la proximite de l'ouverture de la Conference de la sante,  a  decide  d'y inviter sans  delai  des  ob› servateurs  de  seize  Etats  non  membres  des Nations  Unies,  des  commissions  interalliees  de controle  de  trois  autres  pays  et  de dix · organi› sations  internationales  s'interessant a la  sante publique.
2. Le  Comite  a  etudie  une serie  de  questions qui  av'aient provoque  des  observations  dans  les discussions  par le Conseil du rapport de la Com› mission  technique  preparatoire.

3. Par une majorite de cinq a quatre voix,  le Co mite  a  decide  de  recommander a la  Conf e› rence  constituante  de  donner a }'Organisation 'mondiale de la sante  le  pouvoir de preparer et signer  des  conventions  ( section  VI,  3  ( e)  du rapport).  La  minorite  n'etait  pas  opposee a !'attribution de  tels  pouvoirs,  mais  estimait  que !'opinion du Conseil  sur ce  point aurait dil  etre exprimee sous la forme  d'une observation plutot que d'une recommandation.

4. Le Comite a discute la question des bureaux sanitaires  regionaux  (section  XII du rapport de la  Commission  technique  preparatoire)  et  leur relation  avec  "l'Organisation mondiale de sante unique" a creer;  cinq  de  ses  membres  ant  ex› prime le desir que le Conseil recommande !'alter› native du rapport qui correspond a la mise  des bureaux regionaux sous  l'autorite de !'Organisa› tion  de  la  sante;  cinq  autres  membres  prefere› raient  que la Conference  elle-meme  decide  des relations  qui devraient  exister  entre les  bureaux regionaux  et  !'Organisation centrale,  et  que les vues  exprimees  par les  membres du  Conseil a ce sujet lui soient communiquees pour information.

5. Le Comite a discute la proposition faite  par l'un de ses  membres tendant a ce que la Consti› tution  de  !'Organisation de  la  sante  entre  en vigueur en vertu d'une resolution de l'Assemblee generale des Nations Unies en septembre.
items_en: The Drafting Committee met on 4 and 8 June 1946. ·

1. The Committee, convinced of the need for early cooperation from all countries in the field of health and taking into account the proximate opening of the Conference of Health, decided to invite, without delay, observators from sixteen non-member States of the United Nations, inter-allied commissions of control from three other countries and ten international organizations interested in public health.
2. The Committee examined a series of questions which led to observations in the Council's discussions of the Commission's report on the preparatory technical mission.

3. By a majority of five to four votes, the Comite decided to recommend to the Constituent Conference that it give a 'global health organization the power to prepare and sign conventions (section VI, 3 (e) of the report). The minority was not opposed to the granting of such powers, but felt that the Council's opinion on this point would have been expressed in the form of an observation rather than a recommendation.

4. The Committee discussed the question of regional health offices (section XII of the report of the Preparatory Technical Commission) and their relationship with the "Single World Health Organization" to be created; five of its members ex› takes precedence over the desire recommended by the Council !'alter› native of the report which corresponds to the placing of the regional offices under the authority of !'Organisa› tion of health; five other members prefer that the Conference itself decide on the relations which should exist between the regional offices and !' Central Organization, and that the views expressed by the members of the Council on this subject be communicated to it for information.
5. The Committee discussed the proposal made by one of its members that the Council of Health should enter into force under a resolution of the General Assembly of the United Nations in September.

text_fr: Cinq  membres  ant ete  d'avis que  cette  me› thode  n'etait pas  praticable  clans  l'etat de  la legislation  constitutionnelle  de  leur  pays  et ont exprime leur preference  en  faveur  d'une proce› dure  telle  que  celle  indiquee  a  la section  XIX du rapport de la Commission technique prepara› toire, selon laquelle les  Etats peuvent signer avec ou sans reserve, quanta une approbation legisla-
text_en: Five members were of the view that this method was not practicable in the light of the constitutional legislation of their country and expressed their preference for a procedure such as that set out in Section XIX of the Technical Commission's report prepared, according to which States may sign with or without reservation, as a matter of legislative approval.

text_fr: The  Constitution  should  come  into  force when th~ specified number .. of signatures without reservations  and ratifications  has  been obtained. Two  members  considered  . that  this,  number should be twenty-six.
text_en: The Constitution should come into force when the~ specified number .. of signatures without reservations and ratifications has been obtained. Two members considered .. that this, number should be twenty-six.

text_en: The moral credit of the work of the Organization of Health should benefit the United Nations.

text_fr: Le personnel de !'Organisation de la sante devrait, autant que possible, avoir les memes statuts,  reglements  et  echelles  de  traite› ment, etc., que celui des Nations Unies, afin de  permettre des  transferts  et  des  promotions ent.re  ces  deux personnels.
text_en: The staff of the Organization of Health should, as far as possible, have the same statutes, regulations and trading scales, etc., as that of the United Nations, in order to enable transfers and promotions to be made between these two personnel.

text_fr: Le personnel de !'Organisation de la sante devrait  etre  soumis  aux  memes  regles  de loyalisme  et  d'independance  vis-a-vis  des influences  nation.ales  que  le  personnel  des Nlltions  Unies.
text_en: The staff of the Organization of Health should be subject to the same rules of loyalty and independence from national influences as the staff of the United Nations.

text_fr: XVII  2 b) Relations avec les  organisations inter gouvernemental.es  -specialisaes
text_en: XVII 2 b) Relations with inter-governmental organizations.

text_fr: II  ne  devrait  y  avoir  qu'une Organisa› tion  de sante  unique,  qui  devrait  absorber les institutions internationales de  sante existantes  ( Office  international  d'hygiene publique).
text_en: There should only be a single health organisation, which should absorb existing international health institutions (International Office of Public Hygiene).

text_fr: Entree  en  vigueur  de  la  constitution de  !'Organisation de  la sante
text_en: Entry into force of the Constitution of the Organization of Health

text_fr: Cette  entree ·en vigueur  devrait · se  pro› duire seulement apres que vingt-six ratifica› tions  et adhesions  valables  a  la convention constituante  auront  ete  deposees  par  des . Etats Membres des Nations Unies.
text_en: This entry into force should be effected only after twenty-six ratifications and accessions to the Constituent Convention have been deposited by Member States of the United Nations.

text_fr: Deux autres membres ont estime au con› traire  que  l'entree en  vigueur  devrait  etre aussi  rapide que  possible  et que la Consti› h1tion  devrait  etre  redigee  de  telle  fa~n qu'il s~it possible a I' Assemblee generale des Nations U nies en septembre de decider son entree en vigueur sans attendre de nouvelles ratifications.
text_en: Two other members were of the view that the entry into force should be as rapid as possible and that the Constituency should be drafted in such a way that it is possible for the General Assembly of the United Nations in September to decide on its entry into force without waiting for further ratifications.

text_fr: Cinq membres ont cependant juge cette procedure inapplicable dans l'etat actuel de la legisla.tion  constitutionnelle de leur pays.
text_en: However, five members considered this procedure to be inapplicable in the current state of constitutional legislation in their country.

items_fr: 1. The  functions  of  the  International  Refugee , Organization to be carried out in accordance with the purposes  and principles  of the Charter of the United Nations shall be the repatriation, the iden› tification,  registration  and  classification,  the  care and  assistance,  the  legal  and  political  protection, the  transport and the  permammt re-establishment of persons who are the concern of the Organization · as  defined in article I, with a view: .

(a) To encourag1,ng and assisting in every way possible  the early  return to  their country of ori› gin  of those  persons  who  desire  to  return,  and, in  particular,  to  give  them  enough  food  for  a period  of three  months from  the time  of their departure from their present place of residence?

. (b) To  facilitating  the  re-establishment  of other persons in countries of temporary residence, the emigration to and re-establishment in other countries  qf  individuals  or  family  units,  and, as  may be necessary  and practicable, within the resources  that may  be  available  and subject  to the  relevant  financial  regulations,  the  investi› gation,  promotion  or  execution  of  projects  of group settlement.
2. For the purpose  of carrying out its  functions, the  Organization  may  engage  in  all  appropriate activities,  and  to  this  end,  shall  have  power:
(a) To receive and disburse private and pub› lic funds;
( b) As  necessary  to  take  on  lease  land  and buildings, to accept them in gift, to acquire them by purchase  in  exceptional  circumstances  only and to hold and sell  or lease them;
(c) To acquire,,hold and convey other neces› sary  property;
(d) To enter into  contracts,  and  undertake obliga,tions;

( e) To  conduct  negotiations  with  Govern› ments,  eonsult  and  co-operate  with  public  and private organizations whenever it is  deemed  ad› visable insofar as they share the purposes of the administration.

(f) To  promote  the  conclusion  of  bilateral a11rangements  for  mutual  assistance  in  the' re› patriation  of displaced  persons,  having  regard to  the  principles  laid  down  in  paragraph  ( c) (ii)  of  the  resolution  adopted  by  the \_ General -Assembly  on  12  February  1946;

items_en: 1. The functions of the International Refugee , Organization to be transported out in accordance with the purposes and principals of the Charter of the United Nations shall be the repatriation, the iden› tification, registration and classification, the care and assistance, the legal and political protection, the transport and the permammt re-establishment of persons who are the concern of the Organization · as defined in article I, with a view: .

(a) To encourage1,ng and assisting in every way possible the early return to their country of ori› gin of these people who desire to return, and, in particular, to give them enough food for a period of three months from the time of their departure from their present place of residence?

. (b) To facilitate the re-establishment of other persons in countries of temporary residence, the migration to and re-establishment in other countries qf individual or family units, and, as may be necessary and practical, within the resources that may be available and subject to the relevant financial regulations, the investment› promotion or execution of projects of group settlement.
2. For the purpose of carrying out its functions, the Organization may engage in all appropriate activities, and to this end, shall have power:
(a) To receive and disburse private and public› lic funds;
(b) As necessary to take on leave land and buildings, to accept them in gift, to acquire them by purchase in exceptional circumstances only and to hold and sell or lease them;
(c) To acquire,, hold and convey other neces› sary property;
(d) To enter into contracts, and undertake obliga,tions;

(e) To conduct negotiations with Government› ments, eonsult and co-operator with public and private organizations whenever it is deemed ad› visable insofar as they share the purposes of the administration.

(f) To promote the conclusion of bilateral aliens for mutual assistance in the' re› patriation of displaced persons, having regard to the principals ugly down in paragraph (c) (ii) of the resolution adopted by the \_ General -Assembly on 12 February 1946;
(g) To supplement staff, subject to the provi› sions of Article VIII;
(h) To manage undertakings;

text_fr: Ont decide, afin  d'atteindre aussi  rapidement que· possible  le  buts enon,ces  ci-dessus,  d'etablii:, et etablissent  par les  presentes,  un organisme n'ayant ,  pas  de  caractere  permanent  qui  prendra  le ~Qm d'Organisme international pour les refugies et cons› tituera  une  institutio\_n  specialisee  reliee  a l'Or› ganisation  des  Nations  Unies;  et en  consequence, Ont adopte les  articles  suivants.
text_en: Have decided, in order to achieve as quickly as possible the aims, these above, to establish: and establish by the present, an organization having no permanent character which will take the ~Qm of International Agency for Refugees and will establish a specialized institute connected with the Organization of the United Nations; and accordingly, Have adopted the following articles.

2. Pour J?accomplissement  de  ces  fonctions,  l'Or› ganisme  peut  se  livrer  a toutes  activites  appro› priees  et, a cette  fin,  a  pouvoir:

c) D'acquerir, de  conserver  et  de ceder  tous , autres biens  qui lui seront necessaires;

e) De mener des  negociations  avec' les  Gou› vernements, d'entrer en consultation et de colla› borer  avec  des  organismes  publics  ou  prives, 'chaque fois que cela para.it utile, dans la mesure ou ils  poursuivent Jes  memes buts que l'administration; ,

items_en: 1. The International Agency for Refugees shall assume, in accordance with the principles and purposes of the United Nations Charter, the responsibility for repatriation, identification, registration and classification, care and maintenance, legal protection and, political, transport and final establishment of persons shall be based on its competence, within the meaning of the definition referred to in Article I, with a view to:

(a) To encourage and support by all possible means the prompt return of persons who intend to return to their country of origin, and in particular to provide them with a sufficient quantity of food products for a period of three months from the moment when Qs leaves their current residence;

(b) To facilitate the re-establishment and re-establishment of other persons in the countries. of pro-yise residence, as well as immigration and the re-establishment of single persons or families in other countries and, if necessary and possible, to facilitate, depending on the resources available and in accordance with the relevant financial positions, the study, establishment or implementation of group installation projects.
2. For the performance of these functions, the Gianism Gold› can engage in all activities about" praying and, for this purpose, has the power:
(a) To receive and subsidize private and public funds;
(b) To take a lease, if necessary, and to acquire free of charge or, in exceptional circumstances, ~ land and buildings, and to retain, sell or rent them;
(c) To acquire, retain and dispose of all other property necessary for it;
(d) To enter into contracts and assume responsibilities;

(e) To conduct negotiations with the Government, to consult and to consult with public or private bodies, 'every time it seems useful, to the extent that they pursue the same aims as the administration;

(f) To promote the conclusion of bilateral agreements on mutual assistance in the repatriation of displaced persons, taking into account the principles set out in paragraph (c) (ii) of the resolution adopted by the General Assembly on 12 February 1946;
(g) To recruit persons in accordance with the provisions of article VIII;
(h) Conducting business;

items_en: 1. The chief official of the Organization shall be the Director General. The Director shall be responsible to the General Council and shall administer and direct the Organization in accordance with the directives of the General Council and the Executive Committee.

2. The Director General shall be present by the Executive Committee and appointed by the General Council. If the Executive Committee does not present a candidate which the General Coriseil may accept, he may appoint a person who has not been present,› by the Committee. If the position of General Directeut becomes vacant, the Executive Committee may appoint a Director General by interim who will assume all the duties and functions of that post until the General Council can appoint a General Director.
3. The Director General shall perform his functions under a sign contract, at No.;>m of the Agency, by the Chairman of the Executive Committee; this corttrat shall contain.
4. The staff of the Agency shall be appointed by the Director General, according to rules to be established by the General Council.

5. The Director General may attend {or be represented by one of his adjoints) to all meetings of the General Council, the Executive Committee and all the Sub-Committees. He himself (or his representative) may take the floor, his right to vote, during these meetings.

6. The Director-General shall be responsible to the Executive Committee and the General Council for the management of the finances of the IRO, in accordance with the financial rules and decisions of the General Council OR of the Executive Committee.

7. (a) The Director General shall prepare, every six months, a report on the activities of the Organization. A report on two shall cover the work of the Organization for the whole year and shall provide a full account of its activities rapidly during this period. These reports shall be submitted to the Executive Committee and shall be presented to the General Council during the following plenary session, accompanied by comments which the Executive Committee may wish to make concerning them.
(b) In the course of the other meetings of the General Council, the Director General shall present an account of the activities of the Organization since the previous meeting.

items_en: 1. The Director-General shall submit to the Council-General an annual budget to cover the ministerial and executive expenditure and the expenditure which is binding on the major establishment projects of the Agency and, in the event of such expenditure, the supplementary budgets which are necessary.
2. Each Member State undertakes to contribute, without delay to the administrative expenditure of the Gold Treaty, in the proportion which it shall assieve (in accordance with paragraph 1 above).

3. Each Member State shall contribute to the expenditure on implementation and the expenditure on major establishment projects in the proportion assigned to it in accordance with paragraph 1 above and subject to the procedures provided for in its constitutional law.

4. The administrative budget of the Organization shall be at least one year before the General Assembly so that it may examine and formulate on its subject the recommendations which it may deem appropriate. The act by which the Organization shall be connected with the Organization of the United Nations in accordance with Article III above may provide, inter alia, for the approval of the administrative budget of the Organization by the General Assembly of the United Nations.

5. The following exceptional provisions shall apply to the financial year in which the present constitution between e.n.v.u., without prejudice to the provisions relating to supplementary budgets set out in paragraph 1 above:
(a) The budget shall be the provisional budget provided for in Annex II to the preceding Constitution; and
(b) The amounts to be contributed by the m1m1ber · Governments shall be in the propor› tions set forth in annex II to this constitution. ' ' '
(b) The amount of the Member States' contributions shall correspond to the scale provided for in Annex II to this Constitution:

items_en: 1. It has been recognized that no defmition of persons falling under the competence of the new Organization can be generally accepted unless it is interpreted in light of certain general principles laid down in the preliminary and integral part of the defence itself. Accordingly, it is recommended that the following principles be adopted:

(a) The main aim of the new Agency will be to find, in the light of the problem of genuine refugees and displaced persons, a quick and positive solution that is fair and equitable for all interested parties.
-(e) As ]aid down m the resolution adopted by the Economic and · Social Council on 16 February 1946, no international assistance should be given to traitors, quislings and war crimi-

The essential tilche for persons travelling is to 'encourage and support by all possible:μioyens their prompt return to their country of origin, taking into account the principles set out in paragraph (c) (ii) of the resolution adopted by the General Assembly on 12 February 1946. '
(c) As stipulated in the resolution adopted by the Economic and Social Council on 16 February 1946, no internal resistance should be granted to traders, traders and traders.

( e) It  should  be  the  concern  of  the  new Organization  to  ensur.e  that its  assistance  is ,not exploited  by persons  in  the  case  of whom it is clear . that they are  unwilling  to  return  to their countries of origin  because they prefer idleness to facing the hardships of helping in the recon› struction of their countries.

items_en: (d) It should be the concern of the new Organization to ensure that its assistance is not operated in order to encourage subversive or . hostile activities directd, against the Government› ment of any of the United Nations.

(e) It should be the concern of the new Organization to ensur.e that its assistance is,not operated by people in the case of who it is clear . that they are unwilling to return to their countries of origin because they prefer idealness to make the hardships of helping in the recon› construction of their countries.

(f) Dn the other hand it should equally be the concern of the new Organization to ensure that no bona fide and deserving refuge or dis› placed person is equipped of such assistance as it may be in a position to offer. , ·

2. To ensure the 'impartial and equitable application,tion of ·the above principles and of. the terms of the definition which follows, some special system of semi-judicial machinery should be created, with appropriate' constitution, procedure and terms of reference.

Subject  to  the provisions  of sections  C  and D and of the second part below,  the term "refugee" applies to a  person who has left, or who is  outside, his  country of nationality or former habitual resi› dence, and who, whether or not he had retained his nationality,  belongs  to  one  of  the .following  cate› gories:

(a) Victims of the Nazi or ·Fascist regimes or of regimes which  took part on their side  in the second  world  war,  or of the quisling  or similar regimes  which assisted  them' against the United Nations,  whether  enjoying  internation.al  status as  refugees  or not.

( d) 'Persons  who  were  considered  refugees before  the  outbreak  of  the  second  world  war and  who  enjoy  the status  of  refugees  under an established  system  of  international  or  inter› governmental protection or assistance.

items_en: Section A: Definition of the term "refugee"

l. Subject to the provisions of sections C and D and those of the second part below, the term "refugee" shall apply to any person who has left the country of which he or she has the nationality OR in which he or she had previously his or her habitual residence, or who is outside that country and who, whether or not he or she has COD.Serve his or her nationaJite, belongs to one of the following categories:

(a) Victims of the Nazi or fascist regimes, or of regimes which took part in the Second World War, or of regimes which have assisted those regimes in the fight against the United Nations, whether or not such persons enjoy international refugee status.
(b) Spanish republicans and other victims of the Spanish phalangist regime, whether or not enjoying an international refugee status.

(c) Victims of persecution on account of their race, religion, nationalite or opinion. political, provided that such `opinions are not in conflict with the principles of the United Nations enshrined in the Preamble to the Charter of the United Nations, whether or not they enjoy an international refugee status.
(d) Persons considered as refugees before the Second World War and who have a refugee status under an established system of international or inter-governmental protection and assistance.

text_en: The Committee, taking note of the organizations which have expressed an interest in the consuls and considering that other "organisations" are likely to show an interest, recommends that the following principles be applied with a view to establishing a list of non-governmental organizations which may be consulted:

items_fr: L  .  L'organisation doit  s'occuper des  questions relevant de la  competence du Conseil economi› que  et  social  en  ce  qui  conceme  les  questions economiques,  sociales,  culturelles,  les  questions d'education, de  sante  publique  et  autres  ques› tions connexes d' ordre international. . 3 .. Les  organisations qui se  seraient discreditees pour avoir participe de fac;on  indiscutable a des activites  fascistes  ne  pourront,  pour le  moment, etre invitees  aux fins  de consultations.

4. L'organisation  doit  avoir  une  reputation bien etablie et representer une partie importante des  personnes  groupees  travaillant  dans  le  do'› maine  d'!).ctivites  particulier  en  question.  Pour· repondre a cette  exigence,  lm groupe  d'organi› sations peut former un comite  commun ou tout autre  organisme  autorise a prendre  part a des consultations  au nom de !'ensemble du groupe.
5. L'organisation doit  avoir  un  siege  reconnu et  etre  do tee  d'un chef administratif.  Elle  doit avoir une conference, une assemblee OU quelque autre organisme  directeur.

6: L'org~isation doit avoir qualite pour parter au nom de  ses  membres  par l'intermediaire de ses representants  autorises. Elle  doit  pouvoir faire la preuve de cette q ualite  au cas  ou  on le lui demanderait.
7. The organization should he inter~ational in · its  structure,  with members  "'ho exercise  voting rights  in relation  to the policies  or .acfion of .the: international organization.

7. L'orgari.isation doit  avoir  une  structure  in› ternationale, ses  membres ayant le drqit  de  par-: ticiper  aux  votes  concernant  la · politique  de I' organisation internationale ou les mesures prises par elle.

8.  ,  National organizations should normally present  their  views  through  their  respective,  Govemments  or  through  international  non-govern- , · voir' par l'intermcdiaire soit  de  leurs  Gouverne› mental  orga9izations  to  which  they  belong. It would riot,  save  in  exceptional  cases,  be  appropriate  to  include  national  org~ations  which are  affiliated  to  an  international  non-governmental organization  covering  the same subjects on  an  international · basis. National. organizations,  however,  may be included in the list after consultation  with 'the Member  State  conceme.d if they cover a field  which is not covered by  any international  organization  or  have  special  experience upon which the Council wishes to draw.

9. The Committee recommends that the, Coun› cil,  in  determining  the  scope  and  methods  of consultation with each non-governmental organ› ization,  take,  as  a  basis,  the  nature and scope  of activities  of  each · organization  considering  the assistance that may be expected  by the Council £1,'bm this  organization  in  carrying  out the  tasks set  out in Chapter  IX of  the  Charter  of  the United Nations.

10. The  Committee  considers  that  most  close co~sultative  connexion  should · be  established with  the .World  Federation  of  Trade· Unions, which has already applied to the Economic and Social  Council  with  a  request  to  establish, con. nexion.
JI.  MACHINERY FOR APPLYING T:HE PRINCIPLES FOR  PLACING  ORGANIZATIONS  ON  THE  ELIGIBLE ' LIST

1. The  Economic  and  Social  · Council  shall establish  a  standing committee composed  of  the President of the Council and five  members of the Council, 1 who  will  be assisJed  by  the  Assistant Secretaries-General  for  Economic  and  Social Affairs·respectively.  This committee will  review applications  for  consultative  status,  submitted by  non-governmental  organizations,  and  make recommendations  to  the  Council.  It  shall  be known  as  the  Committee on  Arrangements  ~or Consultation with Non-Governmental Organiza› tions  ( short title:  Council  NGO Committee) .

At  its  fifteent!i  meeting,  21  · June  1946,  the  Council decided  that  the  Committee ,should  be  composed  of  the President· and  five  members  of  the  Council.  The  five members  .elected  were:  China,  France,  the  Union  of Soviet  Socialist  Republics,  the  United  Kingdom and  the United 'States of America.

8. En  regle  generale,  les  organisations  natio› nales  devraient  faire  connaitre  leur maniere  de ments r~pectifs, soit  des  organisations non gou-, veme~entales  internationales  auxquelles  . elles sont' affiliees. Sauf  dans  des  cas  exceptionnels, les  organisations  nationales f aisant partie  d'une organisation internationale non gouvernementale qui  s'occupe  des  memes  questions  sur  le  plan international,  ne  pourront  pas  etre  inscrites, sur cette liste.  Toutefois, les  organisations nationales couvrant  un  domaine  qui n'est du ressort  d'au› cune  organisation  internationale . ou  possedant une  experience  particuliere  que  le  Conseil  est desireux  d'utiliser  pourront,  apres  consultation avec  l'Etat membre  interesse,  figurer  sur  cette list e.

9. Le  Comite  recommande  que ,Ie  Conseil~ pour  determiner  l'etendue  et  les  methodes  de consultations ii.vec  chaque organisation non gou› vernementale,  prenne  pour  point .de  depart  la nature  des  activites  et  le  champ  d'action  de chaque organisation dans la mesure ou le Conseil pourra compter sur !'aide, de  cette  organisation pour atteindre les  objectifs fixes  au. Chapitre IX de  la  Charte des  Nations  Unies.

10. Le Comite esti,rrie  qu'il conviendrait d'eta~ blir  aux 'fins  de  consultations  les  relations. les plus  etroites  possibles  avec  la  Federation  mon-· diale  des  syndicats,  qui  a  deja  demande  au  ' Conseil  eccinomique  et social  d'etre consultee .
items_en: L . The organisation must deal with matters relating to the competence of the Economic and Social Council in matters relating to economic, social, cultural, educational, public health and other related matters of an international nature.
2. The aims of the organization must be in accordance with the spirit and the purposes and principles of the Charter of the United Nations. ·
3 .. Organizations that discredited themselves for having participated in Fascist activities in an indisputable way will not, at this time, be invited for consultations.

4. The organisation must have a well-established reputation and represent a significant part of the group members working in the particular area in question. To meet this requirement, the group of organisations may form a joint committee or any other body authorized to take part in consultations on behalf of the whole group.
5. The organization must have a recognized seat and be a member of an administrative head and must have a conference, assembly or other governing body.
6: The organization must have quality in order to leave on behalf of its members by the intermediary of its authorized representatives. It must be able to prove this if it is asked to do so.
7. The organization should he inter-ational in · its structure, with members "ho exercise voting rights in relation to the policies or .afcion of .the: international organization.
7. The organization must have an international structure, with its members having the right to vote on the policy of the international organization or the measures taken by it.

8. , National organizations should normally present their views through their respective, Govementments or through international non-government- , · see' by the intermediary either of their Government› mental organizations to which they belong. It would riot, save in exceptional cases, be appropriate to include national org~ations which are affiliated to an international non-governmental organization covering the same subjects on an international · basis. National. organizations, however, may be included in the list after consultation with the Member State concerned.d if they cover a field which is not covered by any international organization or have special experience upon which the Council wishes to draw.

9. The Committee recommends that the, Counsel› cil, in determining the scope and methods of consultation with each non-governmental organization› ization, take, as a basis, the nature and scope of activities of each · organization considering the assistance that may be expected by the Council £1,'bm this organization in carrying out the Tasks set out in Chapter IX of the Charter of the United Nations.

10. The Committee considers that most close co~ultative connection should · be established with the .World Federation of Trade· Unions, which has already applied to the Economic and Social Council with a request to establish, con. nexion.
J. MACHINERY FOR APPLYING T:HE PRINCIPLES FOR PLACING ORGANIZATIONS ON THE ELIGIBLE ' LIST

1. The Economic and Social · Council will establish a standing committee composed of the President of the Council and five members of the Council, 1 who will be assistedJed by the Assistant Secretaries-General for Economic and Social Affairs·respectively. This committee will review applications for consultative status, submitted by non-governmental organizations, and make recommendations to the Council. It will be known as the Committee on Arrangements ~or Consultation with Non-Government Organiza›tions (short title: Council NGO Committee) .

At its fifteent!i meeting, 21 · June 1946, the Council decided that the Committee should be composed of the President· and five members of the Council. The five members .elected were: China, France, the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics, the United Kingdom and the United States of America.

8. As a general rule, national organizations should make known their way of dealing with the same international issues, i.e. non-governmental organizations to which they are affiliated. Except in exceptional cases, national organizations which are part of an international non-governmental organization which deals with the same international issues, may not be included on this list. However, national organisations covering an area which is not within the competence of an international organization . or which has a particular experience which the Council is desirous of using may, after consultation with the Member State concerned, be included on this list.

9. The Committee recommends that, in order to determine the scope and methods of consultations, each non-governmental organization should take as its starting point the nature of the activities and scope of action of each organization to the extent that the Council can count on the assistance of that organization to achieve the objectives fixed in Chapter IX of the Charter of the United Nations.

10. The Committee is of the opinion that the most close relations with the World Federation of Trade Unions, which has already asked the Council of Ministers and Social Affairs to be consulted, should be established at the end of the consultations.

items_fr: II.  MECANiSME  POUR  LA  MISE  EN  IBUVRE  DES PRINCIPES  A  APPLIQUER  DANS  LA  LISTE  DES ORGANISATIONS  POUVANT  fiTRE  CONSULTEES

1. Le Conseil economique et social con:stitue un comite  permanent,  compose : du  President  du . Conseil  economique  et social  et  de  cinq  de  ses membres, 1 qui seront assistes des Secretaires gene› ra~ adjoints  charges  respectivement  des  ques-, tions  economiques .et  des  questions  sociales.  Ce comite examinera les  demandes que les organisa› tions  non  gouvernementales  pourront  lui  pre› senter  en  vue  d'etre admises  a  etre.  consultees, c;t f era des  recommandations au Conseil. Il est designe sous le µorn  de "Comite charge d'exarni- · ner les  dispositions a prendre en  vue de consul› tations  avec  les  organisations non gouvernemen› tales" (par  abreviation: Comite  ONG  du Conseil).

1 Le rapport du Com,ite  charge d'examiner Jes  disposi› tions a prendre  en  vue  des  consultations  avec  Jes  orga› nisations non gouvernementales ( document ~/43/ Rev.1),  approuve  par  le  Conseil  a  sa  quatorzieme seance, le  21  jµin  1946  (voir page  108), prevoyait que ce Comite  se  composerait  du  President  du  Conseil  eco- . nomique et social  et de  quatre de ses  membres.

A  sa  quinzieme  seance,  le  21  juin  1946,  le  Conseil  a decide  que  le  Comite  se  composerait  du President  et  de cinq  membres  du ·Conseil.  Les  cinq membres  elus  sont: la  Chine,  la  France,  l'Union des  Republiques .socialistes sovietiques, le Royaume-Uni et les Etats-Unis d'Amerique. '
2. "The  Council  NGO  Committee  may  direct the  Secretariat  to  screen  applications  on  the basis  of· the criteria  adopted  by the Council for this  purpose. ·
3. The list of applications so  screened  shall be circulated to the Members of the United Nations by the Secretariat before it is  considered by the Council  Committee.
JI[ PRINCIPLES  GOVERNING  THE  NATURE OF THE CONSULTATIVE  ARRANGEMENTS

1. It is  important to note that a  dear distinc› tion is  drawn in the Charter between  particiP.a› tion  without  vote  in  the  deliberations  of  the Council,  and the arrangements for  consuitation. Under  ,Articles  69 and  70  participation  is provided for  only in the case of States not mem› bers  of  the  Council,  and  of  specialized  inter› governmental  agencies.  Article  71,  applying  to . non-governmental  organizations,  provides  for suitable  arrangements  for  consultation. It  is considered that this distinction, deliberately made in  the  Charter,  is  fundamental  and  that  the arrangements for consultation should not be such as  to  accord  to non-govemm·ental organizations the  same  rights  of  participation  accorded  to States  not members  of  the  Council  and to  the specialized  agencies  brought  into  relationship . with the United Nations.

2. It  should  also  be  recognized  as  a  basic principle  that  the  arrangements  should  not be such as  to overburden the Council, or transform it into a general forum for  discussion,  instead of a  body for co-ordination of policy and action, as is  contemplated  in  the  Charter.

3. Decisions  on  arrangements  for  consultation should be guided by  the principle that consulta› tive  arrangements  are  to  be  made,  on  the  one hand  for  the  purpose  of  enabling  the  Council or one of its  bodies to  secure expert information or advice from organizations having special com› petence  on  the  subjects  for  which  consultative arrangements are made, and, on the other hand, to  enable  organizations  which  represent  impor› tant elements  of public  opinion  to  express  their views. Therefore,  the  arrangements  for  con› sultation  made  with  each  organization  should involve  only  the subjects  for  which  that organ› ization  has  a  special  competence or in which it has a special interest.  In general  these  arrange› ments  should  be  made  for  a  definite  period, reviewable at the end thereof.

4. Consultative  arrangements  should  not  be made with· an international  organization which is  a  member of a  committee or group composed of international  organizations  with  which  con› sultative  arrangements  have  been  made,  except for different subjects than those  for  which  con› sultative  arrangements  have  been  made  with that committee or group.
2. Le Qomite ONG du Conseil peut inviter le Secretariat a examiner les demandes et a eliminer celles  qui ne seraient pas conformes aux criteres adoptes  par le  Conseil  a cet egard. ,
3. La liste  de  ces  demandes  est  communiquee; apres  examen,  par le  Secretariat  aux  Membres des  Nations  Unies  avant  que  le  Comite  du Conseil  n'en fasse  l'etude.
III.  PRINCIPES REGISSANT LA NATURE DES DISPO› SITIONS A PRENDRE EN VUE DES  CONSULTATIONS.

1. II  est  important de  souligner que la Charte etablit une distinction nette entre la participation sans  droit  de  vote  aux  deliberations  du Conseil et les  dispositions  a prendre en vue des  consul~› tions: Conformement  aux Articles  59· et 70, -la participation  n'est prevue  que  dans  le  cas  des Etats non membres du Coriseil  et dans celui des institutions specialisees intergouvernementales. L' Article  71,  qui  s'applique aux  organisations non gouvemementales, comporte des disposition.s appropriees en vue de consultations.  Le Conseil estime  que cette distinction,  introduite a dessein dans la Charte, est  fondamentale  et que les  dis› positions  prises  en  vue  de  consultations  ne  de› vraient pas etre de nature telle qu' elles accordent aux  organisations  non  gouvernementales  les memes  droits  de  participation  aux deliberations que ceux  dont  jouissent  les  Etats  Bon  membres du  Conseil  et  les  institutions  specialisees  reliees aux  Nations  Unies. ·

2. Il  doit  egalement  etre  admis  comme  prin› cipe  fondamental  que les  dispositions  prises  ne doivent  pas  etre  de  nature  a exiger  un  travail excessif  de  la  part  du  Conseil  ou  en  fasse  un forum  ou  tout  le  monde  discute  au  lieu  d'un organe charge de coordonner politique et action, ainsi  qu'il est  prevu dans la Charte.

3. T outes  decisions  relatives  aux dispositions  a prendre en vue de  consultations doivent s'inspi› rer  du  principe  que  ces  dispositions  ont  pour but,  d'une part,  de  mettre  le  Conseil,  ou l'un de  se~  organes, a meme  d'obtenir des  informa› tions  autorisees  OU  des  avis  de la part d'organi› sations  ~yant  une  competence  speciale  sur  les questions au sujet desquelles des dispositions ont ete  prises  en  vue  de  consultations,  et  d'autre part, de mettre les organisations qui representent des  elements importants de !'opinion publique en mesure d'exprimer les opinions de leurs membres. En consequence, les  dispositions  en  vue  de  con› sultations prises avec chaque organisation doivent avoir trait uniquement aux questions qui rd.event de  la  competence  particuliere  de  cette  organi› sation, OU auxquelles elle s'interesse specialement. En  regle  generale,  ces  accords  doivent  couvrir une periode determinee et etre revisables au mo› ment OU  ils  viennent a echeance.

4. Des  dispositions  en  v;ue  de  consultations .ne doivent  pas  etre  prises  avec  une  organisation internationale  qui  est  membre  d'un comite  ou d'un groupe  d'organisations internationales avec lbiuel des  dispositions  ont ete  prises  en vue  de consultations, si  ce n'est pour des questions diffe› rentes  de  celles  pour  lesquelles  des  dispositions ont ete prises avec ce  comite ou ce  groupe.

5. In several of the fields  covered by the Coun› cil  there will  exist  specialized inter-governmental agencies brought  into  relationship · with  the Council,  and  participating  in  its  deliberations as  provided  in Article  70.  There may be  close connexion and co-operation between these agen-\_cies and  the  non-governmental  organizations whose  specific  field  of interest is the same as  or similar  to  that  of  the specialized  agency.  The Council  should  take  this  consideration  into account.
IV.  ARRANGEMENTS  FOR  CONSULTATION  WITH THE  COUNCIL

1. It is  recognized  that the nature of the con› sultations will vary with the character of the or› ganization.  Accordingly, it is recommended that, in drawing up its recognized list of organizations, the Council should so  far as  possible  define  the field  of  interest  of  each  and should  distinguish between: ·
(a) Or.ganizations  which  have  a  basic  in› terest in most of the activities  of the Council, and are  closely  linked  with· the  economic' or social  life  of  the areas  which  they  represen!;
( b) Organizations  which  have  a  special competence  but  are  concerned  specifically with only a few of the fields of activity covered by the  Council;
( c) Organizations which are primarily con› cerned with the development of public opinion and  with  the  dissemination  of  information.

2. Organizations  in  category  (a)  may  desig› nate authorized representatives to sit as  observers at  all the  public  meetings  of  the  Council. Representatives of these organizations, which will include organizations of labour, of management and business,  of  farmers  and consumers, will  be entitled  to  circulate  to · the various .members  of the  Council  written  statements  and  suggestions within their competence.  Such communications will  be  addressed  to  the Secretariat,  which  will transmit  them  to the  members  of  the  Council.

3. To ensure  effective  consultation  on matters in which organizations have special competence or  knowledge,  it  is  recommended  that  those included in category  (a)  may be  invited by  the Council  to  consult  with  a  standing  committee appointed  for  that  purpose,  if  the  Council  so desires  or  the  organization  requests  such  con› sultation.  The chairman of  the Standing  Com› mittee  should  be  the  President  of  the  Council. The representatives  of  the  organizations  should· be  able .to  particip~te  fully  in any  consultations of this kind, so that the Committee may report to the  Council  on  the  basis  of  a  full  exchange  of views. Upon recommendation  of the  Standing Commi~ee, the Council as  a whole may receive representatives of organizations  in  category  (a) for the purpose of hearing their views.
4. Organizations  in  categories  (b)  and  (c) may  designate  authorized  representatives  to  sit
4. Les  organisations des  categories b) et c) peu› vent designer  des  representants  autorises en  qua-

5. Dans  plusieurs  des  domaines  relevant  de la competence du Conseil,  il  se  trouvera  des  insti› tutions  specialisees  intergouvernementales  ratta› chees  au  Conseil  et prenant part a ses  delibera› tions,  ainsi que le  stipule l'article 70.  II se  peut qu'il y ait  des  relations  et  une  collaboration etroite  entre  ces  institutions  et  les  organisations non gouvernementales dont ·1e  domaine  particu› lier  d' activites  est  le  meme  que  celui' de  l'insti:: tution specialisee,  ou s'y apparente.  Le  Conseil devra  tenir  compte  de  cette  possibilite.
IV.  DISPOSITIONS  RELATIVES  AUX  CONSULTA› TIONS  AVEC  LE  CoNSEIL

1. II  est evident que la nature des  consultations variera suivant le caractere de l'organisation.  En consequence,  il  est  recommande que le  Conseil, en etablissant la liste  officielle  des  organisations, delimite  dans  la  mesure  du  possible  le  champ d'action  de  chacune  d'elles  et  fasse  une  dis~ tinction  entre:

a) Les  organisations  qui  s'interes.sent  au premier  chef a la  plupart  des  activites  du Conseil  et qui ont des  rapports etroits  avec  la vie  economique  et sociale  des  r.egions  qu'elles representent;
b) Les  organisations  de  cqmpetence  parti› culiere,  mais  qui  s'occupent specialement  de certains  domaines  d'activites du  Conseil;
c) Les  organisations. qui s' occupent surtout de  !'evolution de  !'opinion publique  et  de la diffusion  des  informations.

2. Les organisations de la categorie a)  ci-dessus peuvent  designer  des  representants  autorises  en qualite  d'observateurs a toutes  les  seances  pu› bliques  du Conseil.  Les  representants de ces  or› ganisations,  au  nombre  desquelles  se  trouveront des  organisations d'ouvriers, de patrons, de com› mer~ants,  de  cultivateurs  et de  consommateurs, auront le droit de communiquer aux divers mem› bres  du  ponseil  des  declarations  et  des  sugges› tions  ecrites  sur  les  questions  relevant  de · leur competence.  Les  communications  de  ce  genre seront  envoyees  au  Se,cretariat,  qui les  transmettra aux membres du Conseil.

3. Afin  d'assurer des  consultations efficaces sur des sujets pour lesquels les organisations ont une competence OU  une experience particuliere, il est recommande  que  les  organisations  groupees clans la  categoric a) so~ent invitees  par  le Conseil a se  concerter  avec  un  comite  perma› nent  designe a cet  effet,  si  le  Conseil 'le desire OU  Si  !'organisation elle-meme  preseyite  une  de› mande de ,consultation.  Le president du Comite permanent doit etre le  President du Conseil.  Les representants  de  ces  organisations  doivent  pou› voir  participer  pleinement a tous  debats  de  cet ordre afin que le Comite puisse  faire rapport au Conseil apres avoir procede a un echange de vues detaille.  Le  Conseil  siegeant en seance pleniere peut, sur la recommandation du  Comite penna› nent,  recevoir les  representants des  organisations groupees clans la  categoric a) afin  d'entendre I' expose  de 1eurs  opinions.
items_en: II. MECHANISM FOR THE IMPLEMENTATION OF PRINCIPLES TO BE APPLICED IN THE LIST OF ORGANIZATIONS WHICH MAY BE CONSULTED

1. The Economic and Social Council shall set up a permanent committee composed of: the President of the Economic and Social Council and five of its members, one of whom shall be assisted by the Deputy Secretaries-General for Economic and Social Affairs and one of whom shall examine requests which the non-governmental organizations may make for admission to the Council.

1 The Committee's report examining the provisions to be adopted for consultations with non-governmental organizations (document ~/43/Rev.1), approved by the Council on 21 June 1946 (see page 108), provided that the Committee would consist of the President of the Economic and Social Council and four of its members.

At its fifth session, on 21 June 1946, the Council decided that the Committee would consist of the President and five members of the Council. The five members elected were: China, France, the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics, the United Kingdom and the United States of America.
2. "The Council NGO Committee may direct the Secretariat to screen applications on the basis of the criteria adopted by the Council for this purpose. ·
3. The list of applications so screened shall be circulated to the Members of the United Nations by the Secretariat before it is considered by the Council Committee.
JI[ PRINCIPLES GOVERNING THE NATURE OF THE CONSULTATIVE ARRANGEMENTS

1. It is important to note that a different distinc›tion is drawn in the Charter between partiesP.a›tion without vote in the deliberations of the Council, and the arrangements for consumption. Under ,Articles 69 and 70 participation is provided for only in the case of States not mem› bers of the Council, and of specially inter› governmental agencies. Article 71, applying to . non-governmental organizations, provide for followable arrangements for consultation. It is considered that this distinction, deliberately made in the Charter, is fundamental and that the arrangements for consultation should not be such as to accord to non-govemm·ental organizations the same rights of participation agreed to States not members of the Council and to the specialized agencies bought into relations . with the United Nations.

2. It should also be recognized as a basic principle that the arrangements should not be such as to overburden the Council, or transform it into a general forum for discussion, instead of a body for co-ordination of policy and action, as contemplated in the Charter.

3. Decisions on arrangements for consultation should be guided by the principal that consulta›tive arrangements are to be made, on the one hand for the purpose of integrating the Council or one of its bodies to secure expert information or advice from organizations having special com› petence on the subjects for which consultative arrangements are made, and, on the other hand, to enable organizations which represent important› so many elements of public opinion to express their views. Therefore, the arrangements for con› outcome made with each organization should only be considered as subjects for which that organization has a special competence or in which it has a special interest. In general these arrangements› considerations should be made for a definitive period, reviewable at the end thereof.

4. Consultative arrangements should not be made with· an international organization which is a member of a committee or group composed of international organizations with which con› resulting arrangements have been made, except for different subjects than those for which con› resulting arrangements have been made with that committee or group.
2. The Council ' s Qomite NGO may invite the Secretariat to consider applications and to eliminate those which do not comply with the criteria adopted by the Council in this regard.
3. The list of such requests shall be communicated by the Secretariat to the Members of the United Nations after consideration by the Committee of the Council.
III. PRINCIPLES REGISTERING THE NATURE OF DISPO› SITUATIONS FOR CONSULTATIONS.

1. It is important to stress that the Charter makes a clear distinction between participation without the right to vote in the Council's deliberations and the arrangements to be made for the consuls: in accordance with Articles 59 and 70, participation is provided for only in the case of the non-member States of the Coriseil and in the case of the specialized intergovernmental institutions. Article 71, which applies to non-governmental organizations, contains provisions appropriate for consultations. The Council considers that this distinction, introduced in the Charter, is fundamental and that the positions taken for consultations are not such as to grant non-governmental organizations the same rights of participation as those enjoyed by the good members of the Council and the specialized institutions linked to the United Nations. ·

2. It must also be accepted as a fundamental principle that the measures taken must not be such as to require excessive work by the Council or to make it a forum where everyone discusses instead of a body responsible for coordinating policy and action, as provided for in the Charter.

3. Decisions on arrangements for consultations shall be based on the principle that these provisions are intended, on the one hand, to place the Council, or one of its bodies, in a position to obtain information or opinions from organizations which have special competence in matters on which arrangements have been made for consultations, and, on the other hand, to enable organisations which represent important elements of public opinion to express the opinions of their members.

4. Arrangements for consultations shall not be made with an international organization which is a member of a committee or group of international organizations with arrangements for consultations, except for matters different from those for which arrangements have been made with that committee or group.

5. In several of the fields covered by the Counsel› cil there will exist specialized inter-governmental agencies bought into relationships · with the Council, and participating in its deliberations as provided in Article 70. There may be close connection and co-operation between these agencies agen-\_cies and the non-governmental organizations whichse specific field of interest is the same as or similar to that of the specialized agency. The Council should take this consideration into account.
IV. ARRANGEMENTS FOR CONSULTATION WITH THE COUNCIL

1. It is recognized that the nature of the con› results will vary with the character of the or› organization. Accordingly, it is recommended that, in drawing up its recognized list of organizations, the Council should so far as possible define the field of interest of each and should distinguish between: ·
(a) Or.ganizations which have a basic in› terest in most of the activities of the Council, and are closedly linked with· the economic' or social life of the areas when they represent!;
(b) Organizations which have a special competence but are specifically concerned with only a few of the fields of activity covered by the Council;
(c) Organizations which are primarily con› recognized with the development of public opinion and with the dissemination of information.

2. Organizations in category (a) may desig› national representatives to sit as observers at all the public meetings of the Council. Representatives of these organizations, which will include organizations of labour, of management and business, of farmers and consumers, will be entitled to circulate to · the various members of the Council written statements and suggestions within their competence. Such communications will be addressed to the Secretariat, which will transmit them to the members of the Council.

3. To ensure effective consultation on matters in which organizations have special competence or knowledge, it is recommended that those included in category (a) may be invited by the Council to consult with a standing committee supplemented for that purpose, if the Council so desires or the organization requests such con› outcome. The fleshman of the Standing Com› mittee should be the President of the Council. The representatives of the organizations should be able .to participate fully in any consultations of this kind, so that the Committee may report to the Council on the basis of a full exchange of views.
4. Organizations in categories (b) and (c) may designate authorized representatives to sit
4. Organizations in categories (b) and (c) may design representatives authorized in qua-

5. In a number of areas falling within the competence of the Council, there will be special intergovernmental institutions attached to the Council and taking part in its deliberations, as provided for in Article 70. There may be close relations and cooperation between these institutions and non-governmental organisations whose activities are the same as that of the institution:
IV. PROVISIONS CONCERNING CONSULTA› PRESENTIONS WITH THE CONSEIL

1. It is clear that the nature of the consultations will vary according to the nature of the organization. Accordingly, it is recommended that the Council, by drawing up the official list of organizations, define, to the extent possible, the scope of each organization and make a distinction between:
(a) Organizations which are primarily involved in most of the Council's activities and which have a close relationship with the economic and social life of the r.egions they represent;
(b) Cqmpetence organizations which are not parties to the Convention but which deal specifically with certain areas of the Council's activities;
c) Organizations, which are primarily concerned with the evolution of public opinion and the dissemination of information.

2. Organizations in category (a) above may design authorized representatives as observers at all meetings of the Council. Representatives of such organisations, including organizations of workers, employers, consumers, farmers and consumers, shall have the right to communicate to the various members of the Council written statements and suggestions on matters relating to their competence.

3. In order to ensure effective consultations on matters on which the organizations have a particular competence OR experience, it is recommended that the organizations grouping the categoric (a) be invited by the Council to consult with a permanent committee in order to this effect, if the Council wishes to do so, or if the Council itself requests consultation. The Chairman of the Standing Committee must be the President of the Council. Representatives of these organizations must be able to see full participation in all such discussions so that the Committee can report to the Council after having made an exchange of views. The Council sitting in plenary session may, on the recommendation of the Committee, receive representatives of the organizations grouping the categoric (a) in order to hear the presentation of its opinions.

items_en: The International Labour Organization is recognized by the United Nations as a specialized institution with the responsibility to take appropriate measures under its fundamental instrument for the fulfilment of the objectives of this instrument.

items_en: 1. Representatives of the United Nations will be invited to attend the meetings of the International Labour Conference (designated by the Tennessee "Conference") and its Committees, the Governing Council and its Committees and other general, regional or special conferences convened by the International Labour Organization, and participated in the deliberations of these bodies without the right to vote.

2. Representatives of the International Labour Organisation shall be invited to attend meetings of the Economic and Social Council of the United Nations (designation d·below by the term "Council") and of its committees and committees, and to participate, without the right to vote, in the deliberations of these bodies with regard to matters on their agenda and for which the International Labour Organization has indicated an interest.

3. Representatives of the International Labour Organisation shall be invited to attend, in an advisory capacity, the meetings of the General Assembly. They shall be given an opportunity to present fully to the General Assembly the views of the International Labour Organization on matters falling within the scope of its activities.

4. Representatives of the International Labour Organisation will be invited to attend the meetings of the primary committees of the As› appear to be genfrale, for which the International Labour Organization has an interest, and has participated, without the right to vote, in the deliberations of these committees.

5. Representatives of the International Labour Organisation shall be invited to attend the meetings of the Trusteeship Council and to participate, without the right to vote, in its deliberations, in respect of matters on its agenda for which the Organization has indicated an interest.

6. The Secretariat of the United Nations shall ensure the distribution of all written communications of the Organization which are members of the General Assembly, the Qonseil and its committees, as well as of the Trusteeship Council, as appropriate.

text_en: Recommendations of the General Assembly and of the Council

items_fr: 1. The  International  Labour  Organization, having  regard  to  the  obligation  of  the  United Nations  to  promote  the  objectives  set  forth  in Article 55  of the Charter and the function  and power of the  Council,  under Article  62  of  the Charter, to make or initiate studies  and reports with  respect  to  international  economic,  social, cultural, educational, health and related matters and to make recommendations concerning these matters  to  the  specialized  agencies  concerned, and having  regard  also  to  the responsibility  of the United Nations, under Articles 58 and 63  of the Charter,  to  make. recommendations  for  the co-ordinatj.on  of  the  policies  and  activities.  of such specialized  agencies,  agrees  to  arrange for the submission,  as  soon  as  possible,  to the Gov› erning Body, the Conference cir such other organ of  the  International  Labour  Organization  as may be appropriate, of all formal recommenda› tions which. the General Assembly or the Council may make to it.

2. The  International  Labour  Organization agrees to enter iI).to  consultation with the United Nations, upon request, with respect to such recom› mendations,  and in due course to report to  the United Nations on the action taken by the Or-· ganization  or  by  its  members  to  give  effect  to such  recommendations,  or  on  the  other  results of their consideration.

3. The  International  Labour  Organization affirms its intention of  co-operating in whatever further  measures  may  be  necessary  to  make co-ordination of the activities of specialized agen› cies  and those of the United Nations, fully effec› tive. In particular,  it  agrees  to  participate  in,· and to co-operate with, any body or bodies which the  Council  may  establish  for  the  purpose  of facilitating  such ..  co-ordination  and  to  furnish such  information  as  may  be  required  for  the carrying out of this purpo~e.

items_en: 1. The International Labour Organization shall take account of the obligation of the United Nations to promote the objectives laid down in Article 55 of the Charter and of the functions and powers of the Council to the temies of Article 62 of the Charter to make or to provoke studies and reports on international matters in the economic, social, cultural, educational, public health and other related fields and to make recommendations on all these matters to the specialized institutions; and taking also into account the responsibility of the United Nations, under Articles 5 8 and 63 of the Charter, to make recommendations with a view to coordinating the programmes and activities of the specialized institutions, agrees to take measures with a view to submitting, as soon as possible, to the Council of Ministers, to the Conference, OR to any other organ of the International Traiorial Organization which may be appropriate, all formal reccimations which the General Assembly may address to it.

2. The International Labour Organization invites the United Nations to exchange views with the United Nations, at their request, on these recommendations and to report to the United Nations in a timely manner on the measures taken by the Organization or its members to give effect to these recommendations, or on all other results which would have been followed up in the consideration of these recommendations.

3. The International Labour Organization affirms its intention to cooperate in all other measures which may be necessary to ensure the effective coordination of the activities of the specialized institutions and those of the United Nations.

items_en: 1. The United Nations and the National Labour Organization recognize that the future development of an international civil service is desirable from the point of view of effective administrative coordination and, for this purpose, agree to promote common rules concerning staff, methods and categories intended to avoid serious unfairness in terms and conditions of employment, as well as to avoid competition in the recruitment of staff and to facilitate the exchange of personnel with a view to obtaining maximum benefits from their services.
2. The United Nations and the Inter national Labour Organization agree to cooperate, to the greatest extent possible, with a view to achieving this goal and, in particular, to:

(b) The procedure shall have an exchange of views on matters relating to the employment of officials and staff, including the conditions of employment, the duration of appointments, the categories of staff, the scale of salaries and allowances, retirement and pension rights, as well as the regulations and regulations of the staff in order to ensure as much uniformity as possible in this field;

(a) To exchange views 'on the establishment of an International Civil Service Commission, responsible for providing advice on ways and means of ensuring common rules for the establishment of a United Nations Secretariat and specialized institutions;
(c) To cooperate in the exchange of persons, where desirable, on either a temporary or a permanent basis, taking into account:

They agree  to  combine  their  efforts to secure  the .greatest  ·possible  usefulness  and  utilization  of statistical information  and  to  minimize  the burdens  placed  upon national governments  and other organizations from which such information may  be  collected.

2. The  International  Labour  Organization recognizes  the  United  Nations  as  the  central agency  for  the  collection,  analysis,  publication, standardization  and  improvement  of  statistics serving  the  general  purposes  of  international organizations.

3. items_en: 1. The United Nations and the Inter-National Labour Organization agree to cooperate as fully as possible, to avoid unnecessary duplication and to use their technical personnel with the utmost efficiency in their respective activities concerning the re-assembly, analysis, publication and dissemination of statistical information. The United Nations and the International Labour Organization agree to pool their efforts to ensure the greatest possible use and use of their statistical information and to minimize the burden of national governments and any other organizations from which such information will be collected. '

2. The International Labour Organization recognizes that the United Nations is the central agency responsible for: collecting, analysing, publishing, standardizing and advancing statistics for the general purposes of international organizations.

3. The International Labour Organization is recognized by the United Nations as the appropriate body responsible for collecting, analysing, publishing, standardising and advancing statistics in its own field, without prejudice to the right of the United Nations to engage in such statistics as long as they are essential for the pursuit of its own purposes and for the development of statistics throughout the world.
4. The United Nations will establish the administrative instruments and procedure through which effective cooperation, in conjunction with the United Nations and its related agencies, can be ensured.

5. It is recognized that it is desirable that statistical information should not be reprinted by the United Nations OR by any specialized institution whenever it is possible to use the information or documentation that another institution may provide.

6. In order to establish a centre for the collection of statistical information for general use, it is agreed that the data provided to the International Labour Organization for the insertion of its basic statistical series and its special reports will be made available to the United Nations as far as possible.

text_en: Article X and Article IV, paragraph B, paragraph 5, of the Constitution of the United Nations for Education, Science and Culture provide that this Organization shall be connected to the United Nations in the shortest possible time as a specialized institution referred to in Article 5 7 of the Charter of the United Nations and shall be entrusted with the functions of advising the United Nations on matters of education, science and culture affecting the United Nations.

text_fr: Applications submitted by States not members of the  United  Nations  for  admission  to  the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cul› tural  Organization  shall  be  immediately  trans› mitted by the secretariat of the Organization to the Economic and Social Council of the United Nations  (hereinafter ·called  the  Council).  The Council  may recommend  the rejection  of  such applications and any such recommendation shall be accepted by the Organization. If, within six months  of  the  rec'eipt of  an application  by the Council,  no  such  recommendation  has  been made, the application shall be dealt with accord› ing to article II, paragraph 2,  of the constitution of the  Organization. ·
text_en: Applications submitted by States not members of the United Nations for admission to the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cul› tural Organization will be immediately trans› managed by the secretariat of the Organization to the Economic and Social Council of the United Nations (hereinaafter ·called the Council). The Council may recommend the rejection of such applications and any such recommendation will be accepted by the Organization. If, within six months of the receipt of an application by the Council, no such recommendation has been made, the application will be dealt with with agreement› ing to article II, paragraph 2, of the constitution of the Organization. ·
